concepts.title: Planes
concepts.conceptBody:
Plane

A plane is a surface such that a line that joins any two points of the surface lies in the surface. The general equation of plane is $ax+by+cz+d=0$. Here there are 4 variables $a,b,c\And d$. We can reduce them to three i.e.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \left( \frac{\text{a}}{\text{d}} \right)\text{x}+\left( \frac{\text{b}}{\text{d}} \right)\text{y}+\left( \frac{\text{c}}{\text{d}} \right)\text{z}+1=0 \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \text{Ax}+\text{By}+\text{Cz}+1=0 \\ \end{array}$

So now there are 3 variables & hence 3 conditions are required to find the equation of a plane.

concepts.title: Intercept Form
concepts.conceptBody:
Different forms of equation of plane 

Intercept form : 

Analogous to the form as we did in straight line, if the plane cuts the intercepts a,b,c on $x$-axis, $\text{y}$-axis & z-axis respectively, then the equation of plane is given by

$\frac{\text{x}}{\text{a}}+\frac{\text{y}}{\text{b}}+\frac{\text{z}}{\text{c}}=1$

NOTE : For finding $\text{x}$-intercept we put both y & z equal to zero in the equation. Similarly for $\text{y}$-intercept, put $\text{x}=0~\And~ \text{z}=0\text{ Z}$-intercept, put $\text{x}=0~\And~ \text{y}=0$.

concepts.title: Plane passing through 3 Points
concepts.conceptBody:
Equation of plane passing through 3 points 

Method-1 

The general equation of a plane passing through a point $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}},{{\text{z}}_{1}} \right)$ is given by

$\text{a}\left( \text{x}-{{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)+\text{b}\left( \text{y}-{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)+\text{c}\left( \text{z}-{{\text{z}}_{1}} \right)=0$ where $a,b,c$ are constants.

To find these values, put the values of 3 points in the equation to obtain 3 equations. And solve for 3 variables.

Method-2

Vector Form : 

If 3 points ${{\vec{r}}_{1}}{{\vec{r}}_{2}}{{\vec{r}}_{3}}$ are given on the plane.

According to equation type 3 we need one point and normal direction. We have points, we need normal. For normal we can do

$\left( {{{\vec{r}}}_{1}}-{{{\vec{r}}}_{2}} \right)\times \left( {{{\vec{r}}}_{2}}-{{{\vec{r}}}_{3}} \right)$

 

which will give me a normal to the plane.

Thus completes our equation of plane

$\left( \text{r}-{{\text{r}}_{1}} \right)\left[ \left( {{{\vec{r}}}_{1}}-{{{\vec{r}}}_{2}} \right)\times \left( {{{\vec{r}}}_{2}}-{{{\vec{r}}}_{3}} \right) \right]=0$

or $\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{r}-{{\text{r}}_{1}} & {{{\vec{r}}}_{1}}-{{{\vec{r}}}_{2}} & {{{\vec{r}}}_{2}}-{{{\vec{r}}}_{3}} \\ \end{array} \right]=0$

in scalar triple product.

Constant form : 

From the last equation, just applying the formula of scalar triple product

$\left| \begin{matrix}  x-{{a}_{1}} & y-{{a}_{2}} & z-{{a}_{3}} \\ {{a}_{1}}-{{b}_{1}} & {{a}_{2}}-{{b}_{2}} & {{a}_{3}}-{{b}_{3}} \\ {{b}_{1}}-{{c}_{1}} & {{b}_{2}}-{{c}_{2}} & {{b}_{3}}-{{c}_{3}} \\ \end{matrix} \right|=0$

where $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\vec{r}=x\overset{}{\mathop{i}}\,+y\overset{}{\mathop{j}}\,+z\overset{}{\mathop{k}}\,$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & {{r}_{1}}={{a}_{1}}\overset{}{\mathop{i}}\,+{{a}_{2}}\overset{}{\mathop{j}}\,+{{a}_{3}}\overset{}{\mathop{k}}\, \\ {} & {{r}_{2}}={{b}_{1}}\overset{}{\mathop{i}}\,+{{b}_{2}}\overset{}{\mathop{j}}\,+{{b}_{3}}\overset{}{\mathop{k}}\, \\ {} & {{r}_{3}}={{c}_{1}}\overset{}{\mathop{i}}\,+{{c}_{2}}\overset{}{\mathop{j}}\,+{{c}_{3}}\overset{}{\mathop{k}}\, \\ \end{array}$

Vector Equation of Plane in Scalar Triple Product Form

(i) Vector equation of a plane passing through three non-collinear points. 

Let $A,B,C$ be the three given points with position vectors $\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}$ respectively and $\text{P}$ bean arbitrary point on the plane with position vector $\vec{r}$.

Points $\text{A},\text{B},\text{C}$ and $\text{P}$ lie on same plane, therefore $\overrightarrow{\text{AP}},\overrightarrow{\text{AB}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\text{AC}}$ are coplanar.

Hence, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left[ \overrightarrow{\text{AP}}\overrightarrow{\text{AB}}\overrightarrow{\text{AC}} \right]=0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \overrightarrow{\text{OP}}-\overrightarrow{\text{OA}} & \overrightarrow{\text{OB}}-\overrightarrow{\text{OA}} & \overrightarrow{\text{OC}}-\overrightarrow{\text{OA}} \\ \end{array} \right]=0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \vec{r}-\vec{a} & \vec{b}-\vec{a} & \vec{c}-\vec{a} \\ \end{array} \right]=0$

which is the required equation.

(ii) Vector equation of a plane passing through two given points and parallel to a given straight line 

Let $A$ and $B$ be the two given points with position vectors $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ respectively, $\vec{m}$ be a vector parallel to the given straight line and $\text{P}$ be an arbitrary point on the plane with position vector $\text{r}$.

Points $\text{A},\text{B},\text{P}$ and vector $\text{\vec{m}}$ lie on same plane, therefore, $\overrightarrow{\text{AP}},\overrightarrow{\text{AB}}$ and $\text{\vec{m}}$ are coplanar.

Hence, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \overrightarrow{\text{AP}} & \overrightarrow{\text{AB}} & {\vec{m}} \\ \end{array} \right]=0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \overrightarrow{\text{OP}}-\overrightarrow{\text{OA}} & \overrightarrow{\text{OB}}-\overrightarrow{\text{OA}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\vec{m} \\ \end{array} \right]=0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \vec{r}-\vec{a} & \vec{b}-\vec{a} & {\vec{m}} \\ \end{array} \right]=0$

which is the required equation.

(iii) Vector equation of a plane passing through a given point and parallel to two given straight lines 

Let A be the given point with position vector $\vec{a},{{\vec{m}}_{1}}$ and ${{\vec{m}}_{2}}$ be vectors parallel to the given straight lines and $\text{P}$ be an arbitrary point on the plane with position vector $vec{r}$.

Points A, P and vectors ${{\text{\vec{m}}}_{1}},{{\text{\vec{m}}}_{2}}$ lie on same plane, therefore $\overrightarrow{\text{AP}},{{\text{\vec{m}}}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{\vec{m}}}_{2}}$ are coplanar.

Hence, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \overrightarrow{\text{AP}} & {{{\vec{m}}}_{1}} & {{{\vec{m}}}_{2}} \\ \end{array} \right]=0$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \Rightarrow & \left[ \overrightarrow{\text{OP}}-\overrightarrow{\text{OA}}{{{\vec{m}}}_{1}}{{{\vec{m}}}_{2}} \right]=0 \\ \Rightarrow & ~ \left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \vec{r}-\vec{a} & {{{\vec{m}}}_{1}} & {{{\vec{m}}}_{2}} \\ \end{array} \right]=0 \\ \end{array}$

which is the required equation.

Equation of a Plane in Parametric Form

Let $A$ be a point on the plane with position vector $\vec{a}$ and ${{\vec{m}}_{1}}$ and ${{\vec{m}}_{2}}$ be two vectors parallel to the plane. Let $\text{P}$ be an arbitrary point with position vector $\vec{r}$.

Take $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\overrightarrow{\text{AB}}\parallel {{\vec{m}}_{1}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\text{AC}}\parallel {{\vec{m}}_{2}}$

Complete the parallelogram ALPM.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \overrightarrow{\text{AL}}=\lambda {{{\vec{m}}}_{1}} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \overrightarrow{\text{AM}}\parallel {{{\vec{m}}}_{2}} \\ \Rightarrow & \overrightarrow{\text{AL}}=\lambda {{{\vec{m}}}_{1}} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \overrightarrow{\text{AL}}=\lambda {{{\vec{m}}}_{1}} \\ \end{array}$

Now, $\overrightarrow{\text{AP}}=\overrightarrow{\text{AL}}+\overrightarrow{\text{AM}}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\overrightarrow{\text{OP}}-\overrightarrow{\text{OA}}=\overrightarrow{\text{AL}}+\overrightarrow{\text{AM}}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\vec{r}-\vec{a}=\lambda {{\vec{m}}_{1}}+\mu {{\vec{m}}_{2}}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\vec{r}=\vec{a}+\lambda {{\vec{m}}_{1}}+\mu {{\vec{m}}_{2}}$

which is the required equation with parameters $\lambda $ and $\mu $.

Thus, equation of a plane in parametric form passing through point with position vector $\vec{a}$ and parallel to vectors in ${{\vec{m}}_{1}}$ and ${{\vec{m}}_{2}}$ is

$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}+\lambda \overrightarrow{\mathrm{m}}_1+\mu \overrightarrow{\mathrm{m}}_2$

Q) Find the vector equation of the plane passing through the points 

$2\overset{}{\mathop{i}}\,+2\overset{}{\mathop{j}}\,-\overset{}{\mathop{k}}\,,3\overset{}{\mathop{i}}\,+4\overset{}{\mathop{j}}\,+2\overset{}{\mathop{k}}\,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }7\overset{}{\mathop{i}}\,+6\overset{}{\mathop{k}}\,$

Solution : 

Let $\vec{a}=2\overset{}{\mathop{i}}\,+2\overset{}{\mathop{j}}\,-\overset{}{\mathop{k}}\,,\vec{b}=3\overset{}{\mathop{i}}\,+4\overset{}{\mathop{j}}\,+2\overset{}{\mathop{k}}\,$ and $\vec{c}=7\overset{}{\mathop{i}}\,+6\overset{}{\mathop{k}}\,$

Equation of the required plane is

$\begin{array}{l} \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \vec{r}-\vec{a} & \vec{b}-\vec{a}\!\!~\!\!\vec{c}-\vec{a}]=0\text{ }...\text{(i)} \\ \end{array} \right. \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \vec{r}-\vec{a} \right)\cdot \left[ \left( \vec{b}-\vec{a} \right)\times \left( \vec{c}-\vec{a} \right) \right]=0\text{ } \\ \text{ Now }\vec{b}-\vec{a}=\left( 3\overset{}{\mathop{\text{i}}}\,+4\overset{}{\mathop{\text{j}}}\,+2\overset{}{\mathop{\text{k}}}\, \right)-\left( 2\overset{}{\mathop{\text{i}}}\,+2\overset{}{\mathop{\text{j}}}\,-\overset{}{\mathop{\text{k}}}\, \right)=\overset{}{\mathop{\text{i}}}\,+2\overset{}{\mathop{\text{j}}}\,+3\overset{}{\mathop{\text{k}}}\,)-\left( 2\overset{}{\mathop{\text{i}}}\,+2\overset{}{\mathop{\text{j}}}\,-\overset{}{\mathop{\text{k}}}\, \right)=5\overset{}{\mathop{\text{i}}}\,-2\overset{}{\mathop{\text{j}}}\,+7\overset{}{\mathop{\text{k}}}\, \\ \left( \vec{b}-\vec{a} \right)\times \left( \vec{c}-\vec{a} \right)=\left| \begin{matrix} \overset{}{\mathop{\text{i}}}\, & \overset{}{\mathop{\text{j}}}\, & \overset{}{\mathop{\text{k}}}\, \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 5 & -2 & 7 \\ \end{matrix} \right| \\ =20\overset{}{\mathop{\text{i}}}\,+8\overset{}{\mathop{\text{j}}}\,-12\overset{}{\mathop{\text{k}}}\, \\ \end{array}$

From (i)

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & [\vec{r}-\left( 2\overset{}{\mathop{\text{i}}}\,+2\overset{}{\mathop{\text{j}}}\,-\overset{}{\mathop{\text{k}}}\, \right)\}\cdot \left[ 20\overset{}{\mathop{\text{i}}}\,+8\overset{}{\mathop{\text{j}}}\,-12\overset{}{\mathop{\text{k}}}\, \right]=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \vec{r}\left( 20\overset{}{\mathop{\text{i}}}\,+8\overset{}{\mathop{\text{j}}}\,-12\overset{}{\mathop{\text{k}}}\, \right)-\left( 2\overset{}{\mathop{\text{i}}}\,+2\overset{}{\mathop{\text{j}}}\,-\overset{}{\mathop{\text{k}}}\, \right)\cdot \left( 20\overset{}{\mathop{\text{i}}}\,+8\overset{}{\mathop{\text{j}}}\,-12\overset{}{\mathop{\text{k}}}\, \right)=0 \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \vec{r}\cdot \left( 20\overset{}{\mathop{\text{i}}}\,+8\overset{}{\mathop{\text{j}}}\,-12\overset{}{\mathop{\text{k}}}\, \right)-\left( 40+16+12 \right)=0 \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \vec{r}\cdot \left( 20\overset{}{\mathop{\text{i}}}\,+8\overset{}{\mathop{\text{j}}}\,-12\overset{}{\mathop{\text{k}}}\, \right)=68 \\ \Rightarrow & \vec{r}\cdot \left( 5\overset{}{\mathop{\text{i}}}\,+2\overset{}{\mathop{\text{j}}}\,-3\overset{}{\mathop{\text{k}}}\, \right)=17 \\ \end{array}$

which is the required equation.

concepts.title: Vector Form
concepts.conceptBody:
Equation of plane in normal plane

If $\text{n}$ is the given normal to the plane then the equation of plane is as follows :

Vector form : 

$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{d}$  …(i)

$\begin{array}{l} \text{where } & \overset{}{\mathop{n}}\,= \text{ unit } \text{ vector } \text{ in } \vec{n} \text{ direction }  \\ {} & d= \text{ distance } \text{ of } \text{ plane } \text{ from } \text{ origin}\text{. }  \\ \end{array}$

Logic : From diagram NP $\bot $ ON & we have to find the equation of plane containing $\text{N}\And \text{P}$

 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ So }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \text{NP}\bot \text{ON} \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \bar{n}\bot \text{NP} \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \overset{}{\mathop{\text{n}}}\,\bot \text{NP} \\ \Rightarrow & \left( \text{NP} \right)\cdot \overset{}{\mathop{\text{n}}}\,=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \left( \overline{\text{OP}}-\overline{\text{ON}} \right)\cdot \overset{}{\mathop{\text{n}}}\,=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \left( \vec{r}-\vec{n} \right)\cdot \overset{}{\mathop{\text{n}}}\,=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \vec{r}\cdot \overset{}{\mathop{\text{n}}}\,=\vec{n}\cdot \overset{}{\mathop{\text{n}}}\, \\ \end{array}$

$\vec{n}.\overset{}{\mathop{n}}\,$ is nothing but the magnitude of $\overline{\text{ON}}$ i.e. distance $\text{ON}\And $ hence the result.

NOTE : In equation (i) $\text{n}$ should be a unit vector only. If you not given the unit vector instead it is given like $\vec{r}\cdot \vec{n}=d$, then at this moment convert it to $r\cdot \frac{{\vec{n}}}{\left| n \right|}=\frac{d}{\left| n \right|}$.

concepts.title: Cartesian Form
concepts.conceptBody:
Equation of plane in normal plane

Cartesian Form : 

Put $r=x\overset{}{\hat{i}}\,+y\overset{}{\hat{j}}\,+z\overset{}{\hat{k}}\,$ and

$\overset{}{\hat{\text{n}}}\,=\text{li}+\text{m}\overset{}{\hat{\text{j}}}\,+\text{n}\overset{}{\hat{\text{k}}}\,$

so equation becomes

$\text{lx}+\text{my}+\text{nz}=\text{p}$

IMP : The coefficients of $\text{x},\text{y}\And \text{z}$ in the equation of plane are direction ratios of the normal to the plane.

Conversion from cartesian to vector form : 

  1. Represent the equation in terms of equation (ii)
  2. If R.H.S. is negative, make it positive
  3. Divide both sides by

$\sqrt{{{( \text{coeff}\text{. } \text{ of } x)}^{2}}+{{( \text{coeff}\text{. } \text{ of } y)}^{2}}+{{( \text{coeff}\text{. } \text{ of } z)}^{2}}}$

i.e. $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{1}{\sqrt{{{1}^{2}}+{{m}^{2}}+{{n}^{2}}}}x+\frac{m}{\sqrt{{{1}^{2}}+{{m}^{2}}+{{n}^{2}}}}y+\frac{n}{\sqrt{{{1}^{2}}+{{m}^{2}}+{{n}^{2}}}}z=\frac{p}{\sqrt{{{1}^{2}}+{{m}^{2}}+{{n}^{2}}}}$

so here $\left( \frac{\text{l}}{\sqrt{{{\text{l}}^{2}}+{{\text{m}}^{2}}+{{\text{n}}^{2}}}},\frac{\text{m}}{\sqrt{{{1}^{2}}+{{\text{m}}^{2}}+{{\text{n}}^{2}}}},\frac{\text{p}}{\sqrt{{{1}^{2}}+{{\text{m}}^{2}}+{{\text{n}}^{2}}}} \right)$ are direction cosines (and not DRs) of the normal $\And \frac{\text{p}}{\sqrt{{{1}^{2}}+{{\text{m}}^{2}}+{{\text{n}}^{2}}}}$ is the distance of plane from origin.

Equation of plane passing through a given point and normal to a given vector.

If ' $a$ ' is the given point and ' $\text{n}$ ' is the normal vector then the equation is given by

$\left( \vec{r}-\vec{a} \right)\cdot \vec{n}=0$

Cartesian form :

For cartesian form put $r=x\overset{}{\hat{i}}\,+y\overset{}{\hat{j}}\,+z\overset{}{\hat{k}}\,$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & a={{a}_{1}}\overset{}{\hat{i}} +{{a}_{2}}\overset{}{\hat{j}} +{{a}_{3}}\overset{}{\hat{k}}  \\ \text{ and } & n={{n}_{1}}\overset{}{\hat{i}} +{{n}_{2}}\overset{}{\hat{j}} +{{n}_{3}}\overset{}{\hat{k}}  \\ \end{array}$

in the above vector equation, then the equation becomes

$\left( \text{x}-{{\text{a}}_{1}} \right){{\text{n}}_{1}}+\left( \text{y}-{{\text{a}}_{2}} \right){{\text{n}}_{2}}+\left( \text{z}-{{\text{a}}_{3}} \right){{\text{n}}_{3}}=0$

NOTE : The coefficients of $x,y,z$ here are Directional Ratios (not DC's) of the normal.Please do not get confuse between the two, that is the reason we keep mentioning it everytime.

concepts.title: Angle of Intersection of two Planes
concepts.conceptBody:
Angle between two plane

Angle between two planes is same as angle between their normals :

Vector Form 

Let $\vec{r}\cdot {{\vec{n}}_{1}}={{\text{d}}_{1}}$ and $\vec{r}\cdot {{\vec{n}}_{2}}={{\text{d}}_{2}}$ be two planes and $\theta $ be the angle between them, then $\theta $ is the angle between normals ${{\vec{n}}_{1}}$ and ${{\vec{n}}_{2}}$.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ cos}\theta =\frac{{{{\vec{n}}}_{1}}\cdot {{{\vec{n}}}_{2}}}{\left| {{{\vec{n}}}_{1}} \right|\left| {{{\vec{n}}}_{2}} \right|}$

If the two planes are perpendicular than ${{\vec{n}}_{1}}\bot {{\vec{n}}_{2}}$ and ${{\vec{n}}_{1}}\cdot {{\vec{n}}_{2}}=0$

If the two planes are parallel then ${{\vec{n}}_{1}}\parallel {{\vec{n}}_{2}}$ and ${{\vec{n}}_{1}}=\lambda {{\vec{n}}_{2}}$.

Cartesian Form : 

Let ${{\text{a}}_{1}}\text{x}+{{\text{b}}_{1}}\text{y}+{{\text{c}}_{1}}\text{z}=0$ and ${{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{x}+{{\text{b}}_{2}}\text{y}+{{\text{c}}_{2}}\text{z}={{\text{d}}_{2}}$ be two planes and $\theta $ be angle between them , then $\theta $ is the angle between their normals whose direction-ratios are ${{a}_{1}},{{b}_{1}},{{c}_{1}}$ and ${{a}_{2}},{{b}_{2}},{{c}_{2}}$.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ cos}\theta =\frac{{{\text{a}}_{1}}{{\text{a}}_{2}}+{{\text{b}}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{b}}_{2}}+{{\text{c}}_{1}}{{\text{c}}_{2}}}{\sqrt{\text{a}_{1}^{2}+\text{b}_{1}^{2}+\text{c}_{1}^{2}}\sqrt{\text{a}_{2}^{2}+\text{b}_{2}^{2}+\text{c}_{2}^{2}}}$

If the two planes are perpendicular then their normals are perpendicular and hence ${{\text{a}}_{1}}{{\text{a}}_{2}}+{{\text{b}}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{b}}_{2}}+{{\text{c}}_{1}}{{\text{c}}_{2}}=0$.

If the two planes are parallel then their normals are parallel and hence $\frac{{{a}_{1}}}{{{a}_{2}}}=\frac{{{b}_{1}}}{{{b}_{2}}}=\frac{{{c}_{1}}}{{{c}_{2}}}$.

concepts.title: Symmetric Form
concepts.conceptBody:
Line and Plane

Unsymmetrical Form of Line 

The equation of two non-parallel planes taken together is called the unsymmetrical form of line because if a point lies in both these planes it has to lie on the line of intersection of the planes.

Q) Reduce in symmetrical form, the equation of the line of intersection of two planes $\text{x}-\text{y}+2\text{z}=5,3\text{x}+\text{y}+\text{z}=6$. 

Solution : 

Let a,b,c be the direction ratios of the required line.

Since the required line lies in both the given planes, we must have

$a-b+2c=0\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }3a+b+c=0$

Solving these two equations by cross-multiplication, we get

$\frac{\text{a}}{-1-2}=\frac{\text{b}}{6-1}=\frac{\text{c}}{1+3}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\text{a}}{-3}=\frac{\text{b}}{5}=\frac{\text{c}}{4}$

In order to find a point on the required line, we put $\text{z}=0$ in the two given equation to obtain

$x-y=5,3x+y=6$

Solving these two equations, we obtain $\text{x}=\frac{11}{4},\text{y}=\frac{-9}{4}$.

Therefore, coordinates of a point on the required line are $\left( \frac{11}{4},-\frac{9}{4},0 \right)$. Hence, the equation of the required line is

$\frac{x-\frac{11}{4}}{-3}=\frac{y-\left( -\frac{9}{4} \right)}{5}=\frac{z-0}{4}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{4x-11}{-12}=\frac{4y-9}{20}=\frac{z-0}{4}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{4x-11}{-3}=\frac{4y+9}{5}=\frac{z-0}{1}$

concepts.title: Distance of a Point from a Plane
concepts.conceptBody:
Distance of a point from a Plane

Let $\text{A}$ be a point with position vector $\text{\vec{a}}$ and $\text{\vec{r}}.\text{\vec{n}}=\text{d}$ be a plane

Equation of plane through $\vec{a}$ and parallel to given plane is

$\begin{align} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \vec{r}-\vec{a} \right)\cdot \vec{n}=0\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left[ \because \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ parallel }\!\!~\!\!\text{ planes }\!\!~\!\!\text{ have }\!\!~\!\!\text{ small }\!\!~\!\!\text{ nomral }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \right] \\ & \Rightarrow \text{ }\overrightarrow{\mathbf{r}}\cdot \overrightarrow{\text{n}}=\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\cdot \overrightarrow{\text{n}} \\ \end{align}$

Perpendicular distance of this plane from the origin is $\frac{\vec{a}\cdot \vec{n}}{\left| {\vec{n}} \right|}$.

Perpendicular distance of $\vec{r}\cdot \vec{n}=d$ from the origin is $\frac{d}{\left| {\vec{n}} \right|}$.

Distance between the two planes is $\left| \frac{\vec{a}\cdot \vec{n}}{\left| {\vec{n}} \right|}-\frac{d}{\left| {\vec{n}} \right|} \right|=\frac{\left| \vec{a}\cdot \vec{n}-d \right|}{\left| {\vec{n}} \right|}$ which is same as distance between the point A with position vector $\vec{a}$ and plane $\vec{r}\cdot \vec{n}=d$ is $\frac{\left| \vec{a}\cdot \vec{n}-d \right|}{\left| {\vec{n}} \right|}$

Similarly, for cartesian form the distance of point $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}},{{\text{z}}_{1}} \right)$ from the plane $ax+by+cz=d$ is

$\frac{\left| \text{a}{{\text{x}}_{1}}+\text{b}{{\text{y}}_{1}}+\text{cz}-\text{d} \right|}{\sqrt{{{\text{a}}^{2}}+{{\text{b}}^{2}}+{{\text{c}}^{2}}}}$

concepts.title: Foot of Perpendicular on a Plane
concepts.conceptBody:
Foot of Perpendicular on a Plane

Q) Find the coordinates of the foot of the perpendicular from the point $\left( 2,3,7 \right)$ to the plane $3x-y-z=7$. Also, find the length of the perpendicular and equation of perpendicular. 

Solution : 

Let $\text{A}\left( 2,3,7 \right)$ and $\text{D}$ be the foot of perpendicular from $\text{A}$ to the plane $3\text{x}-\text{y}-\text{z}=7$.

Line through A and D passes through $\left( 2,3,7 \right)$ and is perpendicular to $3\text{x}-\text{y}-\text{z}=7$, therefore its equation is

$\frac{\text{x}-2}{3}=\frac{\text{y}-3}{-1}=\frac{\text{z}-7}{-1}$

any point of this line is

$\left( 2+3\lambda ,3-\lambda ,7-\lambda \right)$ …(i)

if this point is $\text{D}$, i.e., foot of perpendicular, then it must lie on plane

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & 3\text{x}-\text{y}-\text{z}=7\text{ } \\  \therefore & ~\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }3\left( 2+3\lambda \right)-\left( 3-\lambda \right)-\left( 7-\lambda \right)=7 \\ \Rightarrow & ~\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }6+9\lambda -3+\lambda -7+\lambda =7\text{ } \\ \end{array}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }11\lambda =11\Rightarrow \lambda =1$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ From (i), foot of perpendicular is $\left( 5,2,6 \right)$.

 

Length of perpendicular

$\sqrt{{{(5-2)}^{2}}+{{(2-3)}^{2}}+{{(6-7)}^{2}}}=\sqrt{9+1+1}=\sqrt{11}$

and equation of perpendicular is

$\frac{\text{x}-2}{3}=\frac{\text{y}-3}{-1}=\frac{\text{z}-7}{-1}$

concepts.title: Image of a Point in a Plane
concepts.conceptBody:
Image of a Point in a Plane

Q) Find the image of the point $\left( 1,2,3 \right)$ in the plane $x+2y+4z=38$. 

Solution : 

Equation of line through $\left( 1,2,3 \right)$ and perpendicular to the plane $x+2y+4z=38$ is

$\frac{\text{x}-1}{1}=\frac{\text{y}-2}{2}=\frac{\text{z}-3}{4}$

Any point on this line is

$\left( 1+\lambda ,2+2\lambda ,3+4\lambda \right)$ ...(i)

If this point is $D$, the foot of perpendicular, then it must lie on plane $x+2y+4z=38$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \therefore & \left( 1+\lambda \right)+2\left( 2+2\lambda \right)+4\left( 3+4\lambda \right)=38 \\ \Rightarrow & 1+\lambda +4+4\lambda +12+16\lambda =38 \\ \Rightarrow & 21\lambda =21\Rightarrow \lambda =1 \\ \end{array}$

Therefore, coordinates of $\text{D}$ from (i) are $\left( 2,4,7 \right)$

 

Let $\text{{A}'}\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}},{{\text{z}}_{1}} \right)$ be the image of $\text{A}$,

then $\text{D}$ is mid-point of $\text{A}{{\text{A}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}}$.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \therefore & \frac{1+{{\text{x}}_{1}}}{2}=2 & \Rightarrow {{\text{x}}_{1}}=3 \\ {} & \frac{2+{{\text{y}}_{1}}}{2}=4 & \Rightarrow {{\text{y}}_{1}}=6 \\ {} & \frac{3+{{\text{z}}_{1}}}{2}=7 & \Rightarrow {{\text{z}}_{1}}=11 \\ \end{array}$

Therefore, image of $\text{A}\left( 1,2,3 \right)$ is $\text{{A}'}\left( 3,6,11 \right)$.

concepts.title: Distance between Parallel Planes
concepts.conceptBody:
Distance between two parallel planes

Let $\vec{r}\cdot \vec{n}={{d}_{1}}$ and $\vec{r}\cdot \vec{n}={{d}_{2}}$ be two parallel planes.

Then, distance of $\vec{r}\cdot \vec{n}={{d}_{1}}$ from the origin is ${{p}_{1}}=\frac{{{d}_{1}}}{\left| {\vec{n}} \right|}$ and that of $\vec{r}\cdot \vec{n}={{d}_{2}}$ is ${{p}_{2}}=\frac{{{d}_{2}}}{\left| {\vec{n}} \right|}$

Thus, the distance between these two planes is

$\left| {{\text{p}}_{1}}-{{\text{p}}_{2}} \right|=\left| \frac{{{\text{d}}_{1}}}{\left| {\vec{n}} \right|}-\frac{{{\text{d}}_{2}}}{\left| {\vec{n}} \right|} \right|=\frac{\left| {{\text{d}}_{1}}-{{\text{d}}_{2}} \right|}{\left| {\vec{n}} \right|}$

Similarly, in Cartesian form the distance between parallel planes $ax+by+cz={{d}_{1}}$ and $\text{ax}+\text{by}+\text{cz}={{\text{d}}_{2}}$ is$\frac{\left| {{d}_{1}}-{{d}_{2}} \right|}{\sqrt{{{a}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}}+{{c}^{2}}}}\text{. }$

concepts.title: Angle Bisector of 2 Planes
concepts.conceptBody:
Planes bisecting the angles between the given planes. 

Vector Form : 

Let $\vec{r}\cdot {{\vec{n}}_{1}}={{d}_{1}}$ and $\vec{r}\cdot {{\vec{n}}_{2}}={{d}_{2}}$ be equations of two non-parallel planes.

Let $\text{P}$ be an arbitrary point on the plane bisecting the angle between the given planes with position vector $\vec{r}$. Then distance of point $\text{P}$ from the given plane should be equal

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \frac{\left| \vec{r}\cdot {{{\vec{n}}}_{1}}-{{\text{d}}_{1}} \right|}{\left| {{{\vec{n}}}_{1}} \right|}=\frac{\left| \vec{r}\cdot {{{\vec{n}}}_{2}}-{{\text{d}}_{2}} \right|}{\left| {{{\vec{n}}}_{2}} \right|} \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \frac{\vec{r}\cdot {{{\vec{n}}}_{1}}-{{\text{d}}_{1}}}{\left| {{{\vec{n}}}_{1}} \right|}=\pm \frac{\vec{r}\cdot {{{\vec{n}}}_{2}}-{{\text{d}}_{2}}}{\left| {{{\vec{n}}}_{2}} \right|} \\ \end{array}$

which are the required equations.

Cartesian Form : 

Let ${{a}_{1}}x+{{b}_{1}}y+{{c}_{1}}z={{d}_{1}}$ and ${{a}_{2}}x+{{b}_{2}}y+{{c}_{2}}z={{d}_{2}}$ be equations of two non-parallel planes Corresponding Cartesian form is

$\frac{{{a}_{1}}x+{{b}_{1}}y+{{c}_{1}}z-{{d}_{1}}}{\sqrt{a_{1}^{2}+b_{1}^{2}+c_{1}^{2}}}=\pm \frac{{{a}_{2}}x+{{b}_{2}}y+{{c}_{2}}z-{{d}_{2}}}{\sqrt{a_{2}^{2}+b_{2}^{2}+c_{2}^{2}}}$

*You can see that results of cartesian form are very similar to that to straight lines.

Bisectors of acute and obtuse angles

One of the bisector planes obtained bisects the acute angle between the two given planes and the other bisects the obtuse angle between the two given planes. To obtain the angle bisector bisecting the acute angle between the two given planes, we find the tangent of the angle $\theta $ (say). between one of the given planes and one of the angle bisectors. If $\left| \text{tan}\theta \right|<1$, then the chosen bisector plane bisects the acute angle between the given planes' and the other bisector plane bisects the obtuse angle between the planes. If $\left| \text{tan}\theta \right|>1$, then the chosen bisector bisects the obtuse angle between the planes and the other bisector bisects the acute'angle between the planes.

Bisector of the Angle Containing the Origin 

To find the plane bisecting the angle containing the origin, we proceed as under:

STEP-I. Write down the equations of the given planes such that their constant terms are of the same sign.

STEP-II. Obtain the bisect or corresponding to $+\text{v}$ sign.

For example, if the given planes are

${{a}_{1}}x+{{b}_{1}}y+{{c}_{1}}z+{{d}_{1}}=0$

and $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{a}_{2}}x+{{b}_{2}}y+{{c}_{2}}z+{{d}_{2}}=0$

such that ${{d}_{1}}$ and ${{d}_{2}}$ are of the same sign, then the bisecting plane

$\frac{{{a}_{1}}x+{{b}_{1}}y+{{c}_{2}}z+{{d}_{1}}}{\sqrt{a_{1}^{2}+b_{1}^{2}+c_{1}^{2}}}=\frac{{{a}_{2}}x+{{b}_{2}}y+{{c}_{2}}z+{{d}_{1}}}{\sqrt{a_{2}^{2}+b_{2}^{2}+c_{2}^{2}}}$

bisects the angle between the planes that contains the origin.

concepts.title: Family of Planes
concepts.conceptBody:
Family of Planes

Family of planes parallel to a given plane 

Vector Form 

Family of planes parallel to the plane $\vec{r}\cdot \vec{n}=d$ is $\vec{r}\cdot \vec{n}=\lambda $, because the parallel planes have same normal. Different values of $\lambda $ give different planes parallel to the plane $\vec{r}\cdot \vec{n}=d$.

Cartesian Form 

Family of planes parallel to the plane $ax+by+cz=d$ is $ax+by+cz=\lambda $ because the parallel planes have same normal and hence direction-ratios of normals are same. Different values of $\lambda $ given different planes parallel to the plane $ax+by+cz=d$

Family of Planes passing through the intersection of two planes. 

If two planes are not parallel then they intersect at a line called the line of intersection.

Vector Form 

Let $\vec{r}\cdot {{\vec{n}}_{1}}={{\text{d}}_{1}}$ and $\vec{r}\cdot {{\vec{n}}_{2}}={{\text{d}}_{2}}$ be two non-parallel planes. Consider the equation

$\left( \vec{r}\cdot {{{\vec{n}}}_{1}}-{{\text{d}}_{1}} \right)+\lambda \left( \vec{r}\cdot {{{\vec{n}}}_{2}}-{{\text{d}}_{2}} \right)=0$

where $\lambda $ is a constant

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\vec{r}\cdot \left( {{{\vec{n}}}_{1}}+\lambda {{{\vec{n}}}_{2}} \right)={{\text{d}}_{1}}+\lambda {{\text{d}}_{2}}$ …(i)

Cartesian Form 

Thus, equation of family of planes through the intersection of planes ${{a}_{1}}x+{{b}_{1}}y+{{c}_{1}}z={{d}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{x}+{{\text{b}}_{2}}\text{y}+{{\text{c}}_{2}}\text{z}={{\text{d}}_{2}}$ is

$\left( {{a}_{1}}x+{{b}_{1}}y+{{c}_{1}}z-{{d}_{1}} \right)+\lambda \left( {{a}_{2}}x+{{b}_{2}}y+{{c}_{2}}z-{{d}_{2}} \right)=0$

Q) Find the equation of plane passing through the line of intersection of the planes $2x-7y+4z=3,3x-5y+4z+11=0$ and the point $A\left( -2,1,3 \right)$. 

Solution : 

Equation of family of planes passing through the line of intersection of the planes

$\begin{matrix} 2x-7y+4z=3\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }3x-5y+4z+11=0\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ is }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \left( 2x-7y+4z-3 \right)+\lambda \left( 3x-5y+4z+11 \right)=0 \\ \end{matrix}$

Since, this plane passes through the point $\left( -2,1,3 \right)$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( 2\left( -2 \right)-7\left( 1 \right)+4\left( 3 \right)-3 \right)+\lambda \left( 3\left( -2 \right)-5\left( 1 \right)+4\left( 3 \right)+11 \right)=0$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \Rightarrow & -2+\lambda \left( 12 \right)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 12\lambda =2 \\ \Rightarrow & \lambda =\frac{2}{12}=\frac{1}{6} \\ \end{array}$

Thus, required equation of the planeis

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & \left( 2x-7y+4z-3 \right)+\frac{1}{6}\left( 3x-5y+4z+11 \right)=0 \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & 12x-42y+24z-18+3x-5y+4z+11=0 \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & 15x-47y+28z-7=0 \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & 15x-47y+28z=7 \\ \end{array}$

Q) Find the equation of the plane passing through the line of intersection of the planes $x+y+z=6,2x+3y+4z+15=0$ and perpendicular to the plane $4x+5y-3z=8$. 

Solution : 

Equation of family planes passing through the line of intersection of the planes

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & x+y+z=6\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }2x+3y+4z+15=0\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ is }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ {} & ~\left( x+y+z-6 \right)+\lambda \left( 2x+3y+4z+15 \right)=0 \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( 1+2\lambda \right)x+\left( 1+3\lambda \right)y+\left( 1+4\lambda \right)z-6+15\lambda =0 \\ \end{array}$

Since, this plane is perpendicular to the plane $4x+5y-3z=8$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \therefore & ~\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } 4\left( 1+2\lambda \right)+5\left( 1+3\lambda \right)-3\left( 1+4\lambda \right)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & ~\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }6+11\lambda =0\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\Rightarrow \lambda =\frac{-6}{11} \\ \end{array}$

Thus, required equation of the plane is

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & \left( \text{x}+\text{y}+\text{z}-6 \right)-\frac{6}{11}\left( 2\text{x}+3\text{y}+4\text{z}+15 \right)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 11\text{x}+11\text{y}+11\text{z}-66-12\text{x}-18\text{y}-24\text{z}-90=0 \\ \Rightarrow & -\text{x}-7\text{y}-13\text{z}-156=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \text{x}+7\text{y}+13\text{z}+156=0 \\ \end{array}$

concepts.title: Two Sides of a Plane
concepts.conceptBody:
Two Sides of a Plane

Let $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ be position vectors of two points $A$ and $B$ and $\vec{r}\cdot \vec{n}=d$ be equation of a plane.

Let $\text{P}$ be a point on given plane which divides the line segment joining $A$ and $B$ in the ratio $m:n$.

The position vector of $P$ is $\frac{m\vec{a}+n\vec{b}}{m+n}$.

Since P lies on the plane $\vec{r}\cdot \vec{n}=d$

$\therefore \left( \frac{m\vec{a}+n \vec{b}} {m+n} \right)\cdot \vec{n}=d$

$\Rightarrow \text{ ma}\cdot \vec{n}+\text{n}\vec{b}\cdot \vec{n}=\text{md}+\text{nd}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ m}\left( \vec{a}\cdot \vec{n}-\text{d} \right)+\text{n}\left( \vec{b}\cdot \vec{n}-\text{d} \right)=0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{m}{n}=-\frac{\left( \vec{b}\cdot \vec{n}-d \right)}{\left( \vec{a}\cdot \vec{n}-d \right)}$

If $\frac{m}{n}$ is positive then $\vec{a}\cdot \vec{n}-d$ and $\vec{b}\cdot \vec{n}-d$ are of the opposite sign and P divide AB internally. Therefore, A and B are on the opposite side of the plane (Figure).

If $\frac{m}{n}$ is negative then $\vec{a}\cdot \vec{n}-d$ and $\vec{b}\cdot \vec{n}-d$ are of the same sign and $P$ divides $AB$ externally. Therefore $\text{A}$ and $\text{B}$ are on the same side of the plane

Thus, if $\vec{a}\cdot \vec{n}-d$ and $\vec{b}\cdot \vec{n}-d$ are of opposite sign then points with position vectors $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ are on opposite sides of the plane $\vec{r}\cdot \vec{n}=d$ and $\vec{a}\cdot \vec{n}-d$ and $\vec{b}\cdot \vec{n}-d$ are of same sign then points with position vector $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ are on same side of the plane $\vec{r}\cdot \vec{n}=d$. 
 

Similarly, for cartesian form the points $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}},{{\text{z}}_{1}} \right)$ and $\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}},{{\text{y}}_{2}},{{\text{z}}_{2}} \right)$ are on opposite sides of the plane $ax+by+cz=d$ if $a{{x}_{1}}+b{{y}_{1}}+c{{z}_{1}}-{{d}_{1}}$ and $a{{x}_{2}}+b{{y}_{2}}+c{{z}_{2}}-{{d}_{2}}$ are of opposite sign and the points $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}},{{\text{z}}_{1}} \right)$ and $\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}},{{\text{y}}_{2}},{{\text{z}}_{2}} \right)$ are on same side of the plane $\text{ax}+\text{by}+\text{cz}=\text{d}$ if $a{{x}_{1}}+\text{b}{{\text{y}}_{1}}+\text{c}{{\text{z}}_{1}}-{{\text{d}}_{1}}$ $\text{a}{{\text{x}}_{2}}+\text{b}{{\text{y}}_{2}}+\text{c}{{\text{z}}_{2}}-{{\text{d}}_{2}}$ are of same sign.
